speaker: interviewer
text: Slate.

speaker: interviewer
text: November 16, 1995, Nechama Shneorson, survivor. Interviewer, Charlotte Rettinger, Flushing, New York, USA. Interview in English. I'm Charlotte Rettinger. It's November 16th, 1995, conducting an interview with Survivor Nechama Shneorson in Flushing, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hello.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to begin by asking you to give us your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Nechama Shneorson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nechama is N-E-C-H-A-M-A. Shneorson is S-H-N-E-O-R-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same, Nechama--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --but my maiden name is Santocki, S-A-N-T-O-C-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No. No nickname? And your date of birth, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that would make you?

speaker: interviewer
text: 66. And could you give us the city and country of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The country is Lithuania. And the city I was born is Kovno, K-O-V-N-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What I'd like to do to begin the interview is to have you tell us about your early family life. And if you'd begin by telling us about your parents, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know where to start. But as far as I remember, age of four, five, six, and that age, that I had a wonderful mother and father-- Jacob, my father, Ethel, my mother's name. At this point, I had already two older sisters-- sister by name Szlata--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-L-A-T-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Could you go back and spell your parents' names for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's name Jacob. And mother is Ethel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Szlata was my oldest sister. Ida was two years older than I am. And her name was I-D-A, Ida. And I was the third daughter in the family. Three and a half years later, we had an addition to this family, and again, another little girl. And my father was very proud having four daughters, and her name was Genya, G-E-N-Y-A probably it would be spelled in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a very, very happy family with very, very young parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their ages?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only say when they were killed, my mother was only 38 years old and my father, 40. So at this point, they were very, very young. It was a happy, singing-- constantly noisy from all those girls around, and a happy, happy life. We weren't very rich people, family-like. My father was a hard-working man. He was a butcher. My mother was helping him. There was always help in the house with four girls. A lot of work to do, so my mother always had somebody to help in the house. And every time somebody new came in to help in the house with a different language, we all picked it up right away. So at the age of four or five, we all spoke four or five languages at home already at this point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which languages were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was, of course, Yiddish was number one. Hebrew was number two. Lithuanian was three. Russian and German-- we all spoke these languages.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the primary language that you and your parents and your sisters spoke to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always Yiddish. Yes, that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Hebrew was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew was school. We went all to Hebrew schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were taught in Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lithuanian was the outside spoken language. So we all knew it. And also the school taught us Lithuanian. So we all spoke Lithuanian fluently. And at the age of, I would say I was 10 years old-- which my life started out more constructive, I would say, because they found that I had a, a very good voice. And school started to ask of me constantly to sing wherever I could and wherever they needed me to sing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became kind of a prima donna and a very spoiled brat. Because wherever needed-- somebody needed me, I was there to sing and entertain people. Could you give us an example of who might need, need you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The schools, always in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always in schools.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was always in the premises of school. Any parties that was taking place at school-- somebody was finishing out a certain year in school, or whatever the reason was, they gave parties. I was always invited with a pianist to accompany me. And at 9, at 10 years old, I was-- I felt very important that I had to sing. And I was singing solo.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of music did you sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Hebrew songs--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and-- like the school was teaching us. So it was mostly Hebrew songs, of course. And we were-- my parents were very proud of me. And of course, my sisters and everybody used to come to hear it. And it was a whole thing to do that Nechama's going to sing again in a concert, and so on. And that was a very, very happy situation. And no wonder, because my parents were constantly singing themselves at home. And we always heard music around us on records and the radio. It was the opera. It was regular pop songs in those days that we-- it was the same thing like now, all kind of beautiful music-- and also Hasidic songs and cantorial songs that my father loved very much. Because he used to go every Friday and Saturday to shul. And he used to sing there, too, with, with the cantor and helping out many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no, no surprise that somebody in the house from the children picked it up very strongly, although the other sisters, all of us were singing. And then many times we were singing, like make our own choir at home and sing all of us together.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point, I thought that my life is the most glamorous and happiest of anybody I knew in my class, and until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the war broke out and the disaster started.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back and ask you some details to, to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to fill in. Do you recall the name of the school that you attended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [LAUGHS] whoo! It was called Yavneh. 'Yavneh' is a Hebrew word. And it was a parochial school because it was only in Hebrew. Lithuanians let us have Hebrew and kind of parochial schools--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that teach only in the religion. It was a very religious school. And it's a biblical name, Yavneh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I cannot explain that at this minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there must be a very important answer to it. And it was not a gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still a, a pre-- pre-gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a--

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: --grade school?

speaker: interviewee
text: A grade school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because this is when the war broke out in 1940, when, when I was just finishing at the grade school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the school that I was in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we also had-- in the evenings were a lot of activities--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that we used to get together and make all kinds of shows and theater--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who--

speaker: interviewee
text: --shows. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your family or your friends or--

speaker: interviewee
text: With friends. Friends in school, yes. And many times, even at home, I used to put together, bring together my friends and make a show at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had our own opera. By the way, schools used to take to opera and ballets. So we used-- I picked it up, whatever I used to hear, and do the same thing at home and bring everybody around me so everybody could hear the opera in my own version. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a young impresario.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was a lot of very happy moments-- beautiful moments, beautiful rememberings. As a matter of fact, I have four girlfriends here from the same class. And we-- when we get together, we go back, and we sing the same songs. And we, we make a reunion of way back when what happened then. Those were very beautiful moments. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: To continue from there, in 1941-- Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: --when there--

speaker: interviewer
text: --could, could I just-- I hate to keep doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I wanted a few more details--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of your early life. I wanted to know if you had a kosher home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --strictly kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Strictly kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family was observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were always-- Saturdays-- Friday before Shabbat, my father used to close up the store. We had our own store, a butchery--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and close up. And mother had all the most delicious foods already smelling by-- before I used to come home in the streets, I already smelled her fish and baking her own challah. And the candles were prepared on the table. They were lighting candles. My father used to make Kiddush and go to shul. And Saturdays, it was absolutely no driving, no going nowhere. It was a full Saturday observance very religiously. So it was very important to us. And the holidays, even then, as bad as the business was around in those days, my father used to close up his store for all seven days of Passover and the two days of Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. No question about it. So we were a-- on a, a very religious background.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up before the war, did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't know if I really understood the impact of anti-Semitism, being as I was nine, eight, you know, at that age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when the war broke out, I was only 11 years old. So I-- yes, we used to hear-- going from school sometimes to take my girlfriends, we used to hear Lithuanians screaming, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jew and not, not very pleasant words to us. Or I-- yes, I used to hear from my parents telling stories to their friends what happened, certain situations. So I used to pick up that there is a anti-Semitism. But the explanation, the real impact, what is this all about-- at that time, I don't think I really understood that deeply--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --what that meant. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents active politically in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not that I can-- not that I know of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I know there was always a box at home to, to save money to send to, to Israel. We were always going for that and going outside in the streets and collecting money for Israel through the Jewish people. But otherwise, I wouldn't know. I don't think they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did, did you have relationship with grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember my grandfather when I was about four years old. I remember when he died. I do remember that very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gershon Zivov. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your mother's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my mother's father, yes. And from there on, I had only my grandmother, because my, my grandparents through my father's side were deceased a long time ago when my father was still a young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't know that part of that side of my family, but my grandmother was with us much-- many, many, many times she used to come to us and sing to us beautiful Yiddish songs, and being with us for weeks long, and then go to see her other children, and come back to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she live? Where was her home?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived in also in Kovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But she used to travel from one son to a daughter and so on, and always come back to us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters did your mother have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a brother here in the United States, a brother in South America in-- oh, God, the name of it-- Uruguay, a sister in Africa, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and one brother in Lithuania, which this one I, I knew. That brother, we knew very well. He used to come to us very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Uri El, Uri El. Uri, we can make it-- we can make it a little shorter-- Uri El Zivov. And his wife, Rachel, and he had two children. And we knew each other very well. We used to visit each other very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Were you fond of your cousins? Were they playmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful, just beautiful. We-- summertime, we used to spend with them the whole summer sometimes. That was in Kalvarija, a little city, I would say, probably around 50 miles away from Kovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- is that where they lived or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is where they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it like the country there or--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't country. It was a small, little town--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --little town that they lived there. And also two of my father's brothers lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew my two uncles from my brother's-- from my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, they also lived in Kalvarija.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spent summers there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to spend sometime summers there, yes. We loved it very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the summers, my parents used to take us to vacations. And she used to-- they used to [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] take a house-- not actually a house. It was, like, in a farm. Farms used to have special little home-- little apartments for vacations--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --away really in the country, sometimes-- a real farm. And my mother used to rent a little place like that. And we all used to spend the two months of our vacations in the woods there free, running around like wild little kids. And my father used to come to us weekends. And those were the most beautiful, beautiful summers we always had together-- fresh milk, eggs, running around the farms, and watching how they milk the cows, and running on the horses and wild in the woods. That was my most beautiful feeling-- going in the woods and picking-- my mother used to ask us to pick up some strawberries and-- blueberries we used to pick in the woods those days. Bring the full baskets full to make all kind of goodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So your childhood then-- when did you first hear about the war? How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1941, the summer that I was sent from school with very gifted children together. That was under the Russians already. The Russians came, where-- they, they took over Lithuania--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in 1941. Summer, we were in a camp of children. They were all either-- were good dancers or singing or playing all kind of instruments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a special music camp, they used to call it, away from the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were all there having the time of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could-- do you know the name of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's the-- that is the funniest thing that I cannot remember. And I'm driving myself crazy many times to remember that. I can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you can't. Do you remember where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: The city?

speaker: interviewee
text: --it was near--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --[NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [NON-ENGLISH]. Yes, which was a very famous resort. But that was a little bit further, closer to the city in the woods. There was just a camp, just a plain simple camp for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had an outstanding time there. I mean, it was-- everybody was just going crazy there and enjoying their own things. I was singing a lot. And they were playing. And we made shows. And we made all kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything that belonged in the music world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was 1941 the first time you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. And one night, just while we were sleeping, we heard terrible bombing and, and explosions. And disastrous fright fell in all of us. And we woke up from all this. And our teachers, rather-- what do they call them? The-- whoever took care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Counselors?

speaker: interviewee
text: --counselors--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --told us to get fast-- we should get dressed fast and leave everything behind. Because we have to evacuate the place. Something is happening in Kovno, which we are not too far-- we were not too far away from there. Actually, what was happening is the Germans bombed the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where the airplanes are standing. I'm going to have a problem remembering words now.

speaker: interviewer
text: The airport.

speaker: interviewee
text: The airport. And they bombed the whole airport. That was not too far where we were. So we heard it very strongly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the day that opened-- that opened-- that was June-- July the 22nd, 1941. This is when actually the Germans started to get-- go closer to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard about the war before '41?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- yes. In 1939, our parents started to tell us that something is happening terribly in-- between the Germans and the Russians. And what I understood in that point is there is a war. It didn't bother me because it wasn't-- the war wasn't in my house. It wasn't in our country.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as much as in my childish memory, I remember, yes, we were taught about it. We were spoken about it. But like I said, what, what, what do I have to do with the war? And then we started coming in Jewish people, refugees from Poland. When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1939, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you familiar with that? What did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because every Jewish family started to help them to bring them in until they could find really where to live. So every family took in another family until they got themselves settled.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a Polish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came. And we had a family in our house, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were four of them-- a, a father, mother, and two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have-- you were able to communicate with them? You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish as much as they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because not too many Polish Jews spoke Yiddish. It depends where they came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But with them, we understood each other in Polish and Yiddish. But my parents spoke Russian fluently. And Russian is not that far from Polish. So we, we, we managed to understand each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were they with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they went-- they found an apartment. And they left us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: If it comes to you, you could tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be lovely. I wish I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So this was the beginning of what is a war-- what, what, what war was all about. Retrospectively thinking about it, I didn't-- I'm sure that there was no big impact on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very busy with my own life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any-- Did you know any other refugees from Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would-- did you personally experience any other things that would remind you or tell you there was a war on someplace else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at-- not then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not until that day that really happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until the bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bombing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, So let's go back to that day. You got dressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were-- got dressed. And we ran. We were taken through the woods all the way to Kovno. They brought us into our city where we lived. And I understood then that they brought us into a school. I can't remember which school it was. But it was a school. And parents start coming in to pick us up. My oldest sister came to pick me up. Running through the streets until we got to our house was an unbelievable experience because bombs kept on falling in the streets, too. And it was a nonstop, violent bombing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the time-- by the time we came home, my mother and father were completely destroyed, not knowing what's happening with us until we got-- because there were no telephone communications in those days. So we didn't even have a telephone in the house. But we finally got together. And the family was together. And they tried to explain to me that this is the war. So what are we going to do about it? Well, we'll have to try to run away because if this is what's happening, the Germans will come here. And they will do to us the same thing what they did to the Polish people in Poland. Did you know anything beyond that they had to move? Did you know-- had you heard anything about what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very vaguely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very vaguely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think your parents knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents positively knew-- no, without any doubt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What makes you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe my older sister knew more about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she then?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was four years older then. She was, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --16, going to 16. And she knew much more about it. Either they tried to close, you know, to cover up for the younger two daughters, or-- I didn't know really the impact of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the second morning, we were all packed up. Little packages-- my mother put together some food and clothing. And we started to go on the road, trying to escape [SIGHS] Kovno as far as we could. The direction was towards the Russian border. Try to run away from the Germans if they will come in. At this point, the Germans weren't in Kovno yet. But it looked possible to-- my parents understood that they, they, they are very close to it. And whoever can escape-- to run to the Russian side. Now while we were going on the roads towards the Russian area, we've seen also the Russian Army running away. They actually all-- whoever could-- on trucks, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tanks, cars, anything you-- they had, you could see Russian Army on top of them-- hundreds trying to escape, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were just, like, moving on on foot because we didn't have anything else. We had a couple of bicycles with us. And that's all we had. Were there other people running away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands, thousands.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the roads--

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time an airplane-- a German airplane was flying over to, to bomb, they used to scream, run! Hide! So we used to run into a digit-- what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: A ditch.

speaker: interviewee
text: --a ditch? A ditch to escape. Or into the fields-- there were a lot of very high, tall wheat that they, you know, in the fields.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to run into the fields. And try to hide ourselves there. But many people also got killed that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they drop bombs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So many people were shot. Not bombs-- they were-- they were shooting from the airplanes on the passers-by.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would fly low?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So at this point, it was-- it was a disaster. It was a terrible, terrible ordeal for all of us. My mother started to get very, very ill. She started to feel tired and probably very destroyed from all this. We found somebody on a horse and buggy that-- somebody we knew. So we put her and my little sister-- they tried to help her out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were just walking behind that wagon and trying to hold on. She shouldn't fall off. Because everybody that could, that little wagon that could take, put them on on top of them-- Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and she was in terrible, terrible state.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now what we'll do is we'll stop here. And we'll pick it up on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
